coredata.dc:title: Uptake, translocation and toxicity of fluorescent carbon dots in oyster mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus)
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Riyue
Li, Wei
Kang, Yunyan
Yang, Xian
Qu, Songnan
Zhang, Xuejie
Zhang, Haoran
Zheng, Mingtao
Zheng, Yinjian
Yang, Qichang
Lei, Bingfu
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) profiting from their stable photoluminescence, small size and good biocompatibility have been used in higher organic fields. The relationship between CDs and edible fungi was less reported. Herein, blue emission CDs are prepared by one-step hydrothermal synthesis, and applied on oyster mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus ) for studying the relationship between CDs and mushroom. Laser scanning confocal imaging shows that the CDs could enter into mushroom hypha. The CDs also can transport among the sporophore. The CDs could be nutrition for oyster mushroom hypha growth in PDA-free medium, and the enhancement effect of CDs on edible fungi hypha is dose-response. Using high concentration on sporophore, their growth would not be affected. It shows that the sporophore of oyster mushroom has well toxicity tolerance for CDs. This study illustrates the relationship of uptake, translocation and toxicity of CDs on edible fungi, which lays the foundation for CDs further application on edible fungi system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Edible fungi
Uptake
Transfer
Toxicity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs, HRTEM image of enlarge single CDs and the size distribution column of CDs counting over 60 nanoparticles. (b) The fluorescence spectrum of CDs under 365 nm, and the excitation spectrum of CDs monitored at 440 nm.

(a) The laser confocal images of oyster mushroom hypha incubation without CDs (I), with CDs (II), respectively. (b) The image of the fluorescence transportation of CDs in sporophore in 30 min (left: in 0 min after injection, right: 30 min after injection, red circles: new appearing fluorescence.). (c) The confocal image of longitudinal section (I, II) and transverse section (III, IV), therein, I and III is longitudinal section and transverse section in CDs injection treatment groups. II and IV is longitudinal section and transverse section in control groups (without CDs injection), respectively.

(a) The growth rate curves of hypha in complete PDA medium and different concentrations of CDs in PDA-free medium in 148 h. (b) The colony diameter of hypha in complete PDA medium and different concentration of CDs in PDA-free medium (0.000,0.007, 0.014, 0.028, 0.056, 0.070, 0.140 mg/mL) at 148 h. The different letters (a, b and c) indicate the statistical difference among the groups (P < 0.05). (c) The images of hypha in complete PDA medium and CDs medium with 0.000, 0.007, 0.014, (Ι) 0.028, 0.056, 0.070, 0.140 mg/mL (ΙΙ). (d) The images of hypha in complete PDA medium (Ι), PDA –free and 0.000 mg/mL CDs medium (ΙΙ) and 0.028 mg/mL CDs medium (ΙΙΙ).

head.ce:title: Uptake, translocation and toxicity of fluorescent carbon dots in oyster mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) profiting from their stable photoluminescence, small size and good biocompatibility have been used in higher organic fields. The relationship between CDs and edible fungi was less reported. Herein, blue emission CDs are prepared by one-step hydrothermal synthesis, and applied on oyster mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus ) for studying the relationship between CDs and mushroom. Laser scanning confocal imaging shows that the CDs could enter into mushroom hypha. The CDs also can transport among the sporophore. The CDs could be nutrition for oyster mushroom hypha growth in PDA-free medium, and the enhancement effect of CDs on edible fungi hypha is dose-response. Using high concentration on sporophore, their growth would not be affected. It shows that the sporophore of oyster mushroom has well toxicity tolerance for CDs. This study illustrates the relationship of uptake, translocation and toxicity of CDs on edible fungi, which lays the foundation for CDs further application on edible fungi system.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, nanotechnology is developing rapidly, and much progress had been achieved. However, it is inevitable to release nanoparticles into environment. The effects of nanoparticles on environmental creatures are essential to research, and the relationships between them are indeed needed to illuminate. Like higher plant in the nature, edible fungi are inevitably exposed to nanoparticles. Researching the relationship between nanoparticles and edible fungi is also essential. Carbon quantum dots also namely as carbon dots (CDs), a kind of innovative carbon based nanoparticles with size less than 10 nm. CDs not only have common properties of other nanoparticles, but have its particular advantages (photo-stability, highly quantum yield, multi-colored fluorescence, good biocompatibility and lower toxicity) [1]. Basing on above mentioned merits, CDs were applied as bio-sensor, nano-label, medicine carrier, and nano-photocatalyst in bio-medical field and energy field [2–10]. CDs have a bright application prospect. Therefore, CDs could be a kind of representative nanoparticles for investigating their effects on edible fungi. Nowadays, CDs had been applied into agriculture field. The uptake, translocation of CDs in plant has been investigated, and the effects of CDs on plant are also illustrated. Some studies reported that the growth of agricultural crops could be enhanced after applying CDs in optimal concentration. And many hypotheses had been proposed to explain the mechanism of plant growth promotion by CDs [11–14]. Besides, the relationship between CDs and plant, including the transfer paths, location and degradation ways of CDs in plant, had been illustrated [15–17]. For example, Kang's [16] group demonstrated that CDs can be degraded into hormone analogues and CO2 by horseradish peroxidase in plant. The researches of relationship between CDs and plant have got great progress. However, the report of the relationship of uptake, translocation and toxicity between CDs and edible fungi is rare.

Edible fungi have simple differentiation of structure and they belong to heterotrophs. Recently, the researches of the relationship between CDs and lower organisms mainly focus on micro-fungi (inedible fungi) and bacteria, such as, Candida albicans, Pichia pastoris, Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus, which aim to illustrate the toxicity of CDs to microorganism and the mechanism of anti-microorganism by CDs [18–21]. Bagheri and colleagues [19] studied the toxicity of CDs on yeast, and they found that the effect of CDs on yeast growth suppression is dose-dependence. In their opinion, CDs could close to the yeast cell wall, and then stimulate the yeast producing more free radical to inhabit itself growth. Besides, CDs also could be a sensor for fungi detection. Qiao's group [22] used amphotericin B (a kind of antibiotics) to modify N-doped CDs (N-CDs@AmpB), and the modified CDs could be a sensor for Candida albican detection in food. Most of the previous reports focused on the carbon dots applied on micro fungi hypha. However, most of edible fungi belong to Basidiomycota which is higher class of fungi. Their reproduction is special, which need form basidium. Their tissue is different from other lower fungi. Therefore, it is essential to use CDs as representative to investigate the relationship between nanoparticles and edible fungi.

Herein, bright blue fluorescent CDs are synthesized by one-step hydrothermal methods. The relationship of uptake and translocation of CDs in edible fungi is illustrated, and the effects of CDs on edible fungi growth are also demonstrated in this manuscript. This study presents a simple system for illustrating the relationship between CDs and edible fungi, which indicates the possibility of applying CDs on edible fungi.

N-[3-(Trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (DAMO) and trisodium citrate and were purchased from Macklin company. Potato dextrose agar medium (PDA) and agar were purchased from Huankai microbial company. All the reagents were utilized without further purification.

The fluorescent spectra were recorded by a F-7000 Hitachi fluorescence spectrofluorometer. The transmission electron microscopy TEM and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were taken on an FEI Tecnai 12 transmission electron microscope and a JEOL-2010 electron microscope, respectively. The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected with a Vertex 70 FTIR spectrophotometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were taken with a Thermo ScientificTM K-AlphaTM + spectrometer. Laser scanning microscopy images were taken on anTCS SP8 STED 3X by LEICA.

CDs: Firstly, 0.558 g trisodium citrate was dissolved in 12 mL deionized water, and oxygen could be eliminated by bubbling nitrogen through the solution at least 30 min. Secondly, 3 mL DAMO was added into the above solution for fast stirring at least 15 min. Thirdly, the precursor mixed solution was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene lined autoclave and heated for 12 h at 200 °C. Finally, the residual reagent was removed by dialysis against to deionized water more than 24 h to obtain blue emission CDs [18,23].

Oyster mushroom culture stick preparation: cottonseed hull is the main materials for cultural stick. And other materials are bran, lime and gypsum. All these materials were mixed with 100:15:1:1 mass ratio. The mixed materials was put into plastic bags, and the bags were steeled by plastic caps, and these replete bags were sterilization by 121 °C 20 min. Cooling to the room temperature, the culture sticks are prepared.

Oyster mushroom cultivation in culture stick: After inoculating the spawn into sticks, the sticks were cultured in artificial climate incubator. Before hypha filling up with whole sticks, the light should not be provided. After that, the sticks could be given optimal light and humility.

Oyster mushroom hypha incubated with CDs: 0.000, 0.007, 0.014, 0.028, 0.056, 0.070, 0.140 mg/mL CDs was added into PDA-free medium, respectively. 0.0, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0 mg/mL CDs was added into complete PDA medium, respectively. The hypha was cultured at 25–28 °C for 148 h.
All the mushroom cultivation was performed in SCAU Main Campus Teaching & Research Base.

TEM and HRTEM were used to characterize the CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the CDs show that the nanoparticles size of CDs is around 4 nm, and thenanoparticles ofCD presents theinterlaye rspacing of 0.21 nm correspondong to d-spacing of the graphene planes [24]. CDs have small size which provides the possibility of CDs entering into hypha cell.

The fluorescent property of as-prepared CDs is characterized, as shown in Fig. 1b. The emission peak of CDs locates at 440 nm. The UV–visible absorption of CDs was measured. As shown in Fig. S1. The absorbance of CDs ranges from 250 nm to 600 nm, and it has a peak at 353 nm which due to the n-π* transition of CO bonds in the CDs, and a shoulder peak at around 248 nm, which is attributed π-π* of CC bonds [10].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The uptake and translocation of CDs in oyster mushroom
ce:section.ce:para: For investigating the uptake and translocation of CDs in oyster mushroom, the hypha of oyster mushroom was incubated with CDs, after washing the surface residual CDs, the hypha was observed by laser scanning confocal microscopy. Confocal images are shown in Fig. 2 . After incubating with CDs, the bright blue fluorescence of CDs is observed in the hypha. It demonstrates that the CDs could enter into hypha. According previous reports, Special diffusion, non-special diffusion and special transport are the ways for materials transfer in fungi [25]. Special diffusion and special transport all need specific transport proteins to recognize and support the substances transfer. Non-special diffusion depends on the material sizes. However, previous report illustrated that the size of quantum dots is not the unique factor for the uptake of quantum dots in hypha. For example the CdSe quantum dots, which size is around 2–3 nm, could not be absorbed into hypha cell without amine acid modification on its surface [26]. Other report also suggested that the quantum dots without organic components could not enter into hypha cell [27]. The reason of the as-prepared CDs entering into hypha cell also is investigated. From FTIR spectrum of CDs (Fig.S2), the as-prepared CDs have abundant surface groups. The peak at 3000-3500 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibration of –OH and –NH [28], and the peaks emerge at 2700-3000 cm−1 are attributed to vibration of C–H group [28,29]. The transmittance peak at 1653 cm−1 is attributed to vibration of –CO [30]. These groups make the CDs could be recognized as organic materials by hypha cell, which could be a reason for CDs entrance in hypha. Furthermore, the as-prepared CDs were also applied on oyster mushroom sporophore for investigating the translocation status of CDs. Firstly, the CDs were injected into oyster mushroom sporophore and the fluorescence of CDs was observed for 30 min after injection. The location of fluorescence of CDs in sporophore is shown in Fig. 2b, the fluorescence of CDs was observed obvious diffusion in sporophore in 30 min after injection. Secondly, for further investigating the diffusion of CDs in sporophore, the confocal microscopy as used to observed the CDs in sporophore. As shown in Fig. 2c, the fluorescence of CDs can be observed in hypha in longitudinal and horizontal slices, which demonstrates that the CDs also can enter into sporophore hypha and translocate in the sporophore.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of CDs on oyster mushroom
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of CDs on edible fungi growth are also investigated for illustrated the possibility of application on mushroom. In the study of the uptake and translocation of CDs in mushroom, the fluorescence of CDs in or on mushroom was quenched. Therefore, a hypothesis is proposed that the CDs could be used as nutrition for edible fungi growth. Firstly, mushroom hypha was co-cultured with CDs in PDA-free medium (nutrition-free medium). The hypha growth was recorded every 12 h. The trend of hypha growth is presented in Fig. 3 . The hypha in complete PDA medium has the fastest growth rate and the densest hypha, while the hypha in 0.000 mg/mL CDs PDA-free medium without CDs has the lowest growth rate and rare hypha. Interestingly, the growth rates of hypha in the PDA-free mediums with different concentrations CDs are higher than that in 0.000 mg/mL CDs PDA-free medium and lower than that in complete PDA medium(Fig. 3a), besides, the PDA-free medium with 0.028 mg/mL CDs is the optimal medium for hypha growth (Fig. 3b). Basing on XPS analysis (Fig. S3), the CDs has C, H, O and N elements, and their chemical bonds illustrate that the CDs have the property of organic materials, also other similar CDs are regarded as organic compounds in previous reports [31]. The mushroom is heterotroph which breaks down the organic material for its growth. Therefore, the organic property of CDs could explain above results. Although the hypha colony area diameter of 0.028 mg/mL CDs medium is similar to that of complete PDA medium, while the hypha in CDs medium is sparser than that in complete PDA medium. It is well known that the different mushroom require different medium component for itself well growth and optimal development [32–34]. Requiring specific medium for mushroom growth well could explain that the lower vigor of hypha in CDs medium. Basing on above discussion, the CDs could be utilized by mushroom like other organic nutrition, but the nutrition formula from CDs is not the optimal and suitable one for fungi hypha vigor growth.

In addition, the toxicity of CDs on mushroom hypha and sporophore is also investigated. As reported in previous studies, the toxicity of CDs on plant is dose-dependence, the application concentrations of various CDs on different plant have different threshold [12,13]. If the application concentration of CDs is over the threshold, the plant growth would be suppressed. Similarly, the toxicity of CDs on mushroom hypha also has dose-response. As presented in Fig. S3, mushroom hypha was co-cultured with 0.0, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0 mg/mL CDs in complete PDA medium, respectively. The result shows that the growth of hypha is not been suppressed in the medium with the 0.2 and 0.5 mg/mL CDs, while inhibition appears in the medium with 1.0–2.0 mg/mL CDs. Interestingly, the mushroom sporophore treated by injecting 10.0 mg/mL CDs could growth in normal way in 46 h (Fig. S3b). The toxicity effects of CDs on hypha and sporophore are different, the sporophore could tolerate higher concentration of CD, and the hypha is more sensitive to CDs.

Blue fluorescent CDs are synthesized and applied on oyster mushroom. The as-prepared CDs could enter into mushroom hypha and transport among sporophore because of their physical and chemical properties. The small size of CDs provides the possibility for penetrating the hypha cell wall and transporting among the sporophore cell, also their abundant surface groups make them enter into cell more simpler and transport among sporophore more easily, which indicates that the CDs could be a candidate for biomarker in edible fungi system. Furthermore, the surface groups and organic properties of CDs make them could be the nutrition for mushroom hypha growth. Besides, the toxicity effect of CDs on mushroom hypha is dose-response, the growth of hypha is suppressed when the concentration of CDs over the 1 mg/mL. However, the oyster mushroom sporophore has less sensitive to CDs, it can growth and develop in normal way even though using higher concentration CDs. The investigation of uptake, transportation, and toxicity of CDs on edible fungi suggests the application possibility of CDs acting as biomarker and nutrition provider in edible fungi system.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21671070), the Project of GDUPS (2018) for Prof. Bingfu LEI, the National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFB0407902), and the Guangdong Provincial Special Fund for Modern Agriculture Industry Technology Innovation Teams (No. 2020KJ122).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2021.118010.

Riyue Dong carried out the experimental part, analyzed the data and wrote the majority of the manuscript text. Wei Li performed the measurements and data analysis. Yuanyan Kang, Xian Yang, Haoran Zhang and Songnan Qu provided useful suggestions and discussion concerning this manuscript. Xuejie Zhang provided the synthesis method for the nanomateirls. Mingtao Zheng modified the pictures in the manuscript. Yinjian Zheng and Qichang Yang contributed a lot during revising this manuscript. Bingfu Lei provided the idea for this work and acted as the project administrator.
